regulationSummary.overview: Mississippi has no gold deposits and is not a gold prospecting destination. The state is entirely underlain by Cretaceous and younger sedimentary rocks of the Gulf Coastal Plain — no gold-bearing basement geology is exposed anywhere in Mississippi. The state's mineral resources consist primarily of gravel, sand, clay, and oil/gas. This entry is included for completeness.
keyRules: Mississippi does not have a recreational gold prospecting permit system — there are no gold deposits in the state.
In-stream work in Mississippi waterways is regulated by the Mississippi Department of Environmental Quality (MDEQ) and Army Corps of Engineers.
Bienville National Forest and DeSoto National Forest land allows minimal personal mineral collecting under USFS rules.
regulationSummary.hpaInfo: Not applicable — there are no gold-bearing streams in Mississippi.
regulationSummary.suctionDredgeStatus: Not applicable — Mississippi has no productive placer gold streams.
regulationSummary.handPanning: Gold panning in Mississippi streams is not a viable activity — the state has no gold-bearing geology. Mississippi offers good fossil collecting (especially marine invertebrates in Cretaceous chalk outcrops in the northeast part of the state) but no gold prospecting opportunities.
regulationSummary.disclaimer: Regulations change. Verify current rules at the official sources listed below before going out.

officialResources.title: Mississippi Office of Geology
officialResources.description: State geological survey — Mississippi has no gold-bearing geology; the survey covers oil, gas, clay, and gravel.

officialResources.title: Mississippi Department of Environmental Quality
officialResources.description: State environmental agency managing Mississippi's environmental resources and waterway regulations.
